signals.title: Draw Probability Systematic Underestimation
signals.issue: Model assigns mean draw probability of 22.0% across group-stage fixtures. Observed WC26 draw rate is 31.4% (11 of 35 settled matches). Gap: +9.4 percentage points. Every settled draw was graded MISS or SURPRISE — the model has never rated a draw its most likely outcome (0 of 11).
signals.evidence: 11/35 settled matches ended in draws (31.4%). Mean predicted draw prob: 22.0%. 5 of 11 strong favorites (≥70% win probability) drew instead of winning. Worst case: Spain vs Cape Verde — model assigned 94% Spain win, result was 0-0 draw (Brier 1.786). Root causes: (1) Dixon-Coles rho under-tuned for WC tournament conditions, (2) Platt calibration trained on general international data with lower draw rate (~24-26%), (3) Elo ratings overweight talent vs WC tactical context.
signals.action: Evaluated 8 challenger model configurations: rho inflation (×1.5, ×3), draw prior inflation (+30%, +50%, +70%), Elo cap at 400 points, and combinations. Significance framework uses paired Brier differences and bootstrap confidence intervals (B=10,000).
signals.result: Re-evaluated at n=35 (2026-06-21). Best challenger (H: Rho×3 + Draw Prior +30% + Elo Cap 400pt) improves mean Brier by only 0.012 (champion 0.590 → 0.578), down from 0.034 at n=21 — within run-to-run variance. It narrows the draw gap but regresses on decisive home/away outcomes.
signals.promotionRationale: Best-challenger improvement (0.012 Brier) is not separable from zero at n=35 — within run-to-run variance. Champion v1.0.0-platt retained pending a stable, statistically significant edge over a substantially larger settled sample.
